input_path: data\papers\Heithoff et al. - A Digital Shadow for Accurate Robot Motion Control Integrating Data with Friction Models.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The system under study is a serial robot MABI MAX 100 with six axes, comprising motors and gearboxes for precision manufacturing applications.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin provides real-time data visualization through a dashboard interface and optimizes motion accuracy by modeling and simulating friction dynamics in the robot's first axis using executable simulation models.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: The Digital Twin implementation utilizes Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) with SysML for systems modeling across abstraction levels (requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, product/technical layer), methodologies such as RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics), OOSEM, SPES, and SMArDT for complexity management, architecture description languages (ADLs) including ACME, AADL, MontiArc, and SysML IBDs for component & connector modeling, along with simulation tools like MathWorks Simulink and general-purpose programming languages (C++, Java) for detailed behavior description and integration.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: Computational models include SysML for structural representation (IBDs), ADLs (ACME, AADL, MontiArc) for component & connector architecture, and behavioral models via state machines (SysML v1.7) and MontiArc Automaton. Analytical models are implemented using MathWorks Simulink and general-purpose languages (C++, Java). Data components consist of contextual data traces aggregated from physical systems, with model relationships defined through component interfaces and interconnections per ADL specifications. Active DT components process these data traces for real-time decision-making.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: The physical actuators consist of a permanent magnet synchronous motor driving the first axis (current IM as input, torque τM as output), a gear transmission (torque τG), and a cycloidal gear (torque τout). Control is managed by a Siemens SINUMERIK 840d sl CNC controller supporting separate axis control via position, speed, or acceleration inputs. Safety constraints include a working range of [-90°, 90°] and maximum speed |q˙max| = 75°/s.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: Sensing infrastructure includes two position encoders per drive (motor-mounted and secondary behind gearbox), with sensor data (input current and output angle) streamed via MQTT. The dual-encoder arrangement reduces backlash and positional uncertainty by analyzing differences to infer drive train elasticity and friction.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: Model fidelity is validated through comparison of simulated data against measured data to detect friction loss in the robot's first axis. Data traceability ensures all data is linked to its origin and computational method, enabling integrated dashboard visualization. Accuracy limitations exist for high-precision applications, as the system is less precise than traditional machine tools.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Sensor data such as input current and output angle are streamed via MQTT protocol in a one-way data flow from physical to virtual components, with data traces aggregated and abstracted for specific purposes.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: No virtual to physical interaction occurs; the digital shadow functions as a passive entity with a one-way data flow, thus no control commands, validation, feedback, or execution confirmation mechanisms are present.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: MQTT protocol is used for streaming sensor data (time series) from physical components, with the Siemens SINUMERIK 840D sl controller managing physical system interfaces.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The engineering process utilizes Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) methodologies including RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics), OOSEM, SPES, and SMArDT to structure system decomposition across abstraction levels (requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, product/technical layer). Architecture Description Languages (ADLs) such as SysML IBDs, ACME, AADL, and MontiArc model components, interfaces, interconnections, and composition, with behavior described via MontiArc Automaton or SysML state machines. Behavior detail is achieved through integration with MathWorks Simulink, simulation tools, and general-purpose programming languages (C++, Java).
extracted_characteristics.standardization: Compliance with industry standards including SysML [17], ACME [23], AADL [24], and MontiArc [25], [26] for architecture description and system modeling, ensuring interoperability among heterogeneous modeling tools and stakeholder perspectives.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
conclude in Sec. VII.


II. B ACKGROUND

To better understand our approach, we describe relevant
modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_



**Fig. 1:** Digital shadow reference model as a UML class
diagram. It defines important concepts for digital shadows in
manufacturing.

block_1_judge.reasoning: The extracted description accurately identifies the specific robot model (MABI MAX 100), its configuration (six-axis serial robot), and its main components (motors and gearboxes) used for manufacturing. This is strongly supported by the text describing the use case. Quote: 'The focus of this paper is the serial robot MABI MAX 100 with six axes' and 'The asset of interest is the first axis of the MABI robot, consisting of a motor and two gearboxes'.

The extraction accurately describes the dashboard visualization and the optimization of motion accuracy via friction simulation. Supported by quotes: 'implementing Asset Administration Shells (AAS), and live-data linking as well as visualization in a dashboard' and 'executable digital shadow... to improve the robot control’s accuracy'. It captures the core services well, though it omits details like the specific focus on the 'first axis'.

The extraction is a highly accurate summary of the tools and methodologies listed in Section II.A. It correctly identifies MBSE, SysML, RFLP, OOSEM, SPES, SMArDT, ACME, AADL, MontiArc, SysML IBDs, Simulink, C++, and Java as tools used for modeling, simulation, and behavior description.

However, they do not provide specific details regarding synchronization frequencies, latency requirements, temporal granularity needs, or specific time scales for services as defined in the characteristic description. The text describes a single digital shadow instance for a specific robot axis (MABI MAX 100), mentioning 'multiple data series' and different model types (Engineering, Data Calculation, Simulation). Therefore, the extraction accurately states the information is not in the document.

While the document presents a 'Digital Shadow Reference Model' and a 'concrete realization' involving components like Purpose, Asset, and Processing, it does not describe the overall orchestration and coordination of a broader Digital Twin system in terms of complex architecture patterns, component integration across diverse systems, or system-wide resource management. The focus is on the construction of a specific shadow. The extraction is correct.

The document mentions 'live-data linking' and data being streamed via 'MQTT' to a dashboard, as well as the implementation of 'Asset Administration Shells (AAS)'. However, it does not provide details on integration with external systems, interoperability standards, dependencies on external systems, or integration patterns with other Digital Twins or enterprise systems. The extraction correctly identifies this as missing.

block_6_judge.reasoning: The source documents focus on the technical implementation of a digital shadow for robot motion control, including data tracing and model integration. There is no mention of data ownership policies, privacy protection measures, consent management, data anonymization, or data sharing agreements. The concept of 'traceability' mentioned in the text refers to data provenance (origin and computation method), not legal ownership or privacy.

The documents provide a detailed description of the robot's mechanical structure, friction models, and control system. While the text briefly mentions a 'safety cell' that restricts the working range ('The safety cell of the robot restricts the maximum working range to [−90°, 90°]'), it does not provide relevant information regarding cybersecurity protocols, access control, authentication, threat mitigation strategies, or fail-safe mechanisms as defined by the characteristic description.
